file.content:
# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.

In the future we I expect we may want to create a "notes"
database to better manage the information, TBD.

Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## jj tips

For users new to jj see [jj-tips.md](jj-tips.md).

```
## Chores format

Filename: "Chores-XX-.md"
example: chores-01.md

Format of section labels: "## <short description> (YYYYMMDD X.Y.Z)"
example: "## Topic format description (20260322 0.1.0)"

Example chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Do something (20260322 1.3.1)

Describe something
```

## Versioning during development

This is using jujustiu, jj + git and we'll see how it goes. Below is my
git workflow, jj will be different but we'll have to discover that as
we go.

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump directly to
  `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-devN`, implement
  across multiple commits, final commit removes `-devN`.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before starting.

For multi-step:
1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-devN` with a plan and commit as a chore marker
2. Implement in one or more `-devN` commits (bump N as needed)
3. Final commit removes `-devN`, updates todo/chores — this is the "done" marker

The final release commit (without `-devN`) signals completion rather than amending
prior commits. This keeps the git history readable and makes it easy to see which
commits were exploratory vs final.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: chores-01bugs.md#bug-y

file.content:
# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Have claude code design a claude-code session viewer

  In `hwr.claude/` is a .jsonl file with a set of json lines representing me asking
  claude-code to "reqaquaint" (sp: reaquaint) itself with a trivial app created
  using, `cargo new hwr` and `hwr.claude/` is the directory `~/.claude/projects/<path>/`.

  Medium term want to convert the jsonl lines into a the "conversation".
  In the short term I'd like to create the rust structs that each represent
  the information, I don't want to process it I just want to be able to
  use serde to deserialize the data in the file into the set of rust structs.
  
  I think each line should destruct into a single struct, but I suspect
  this "super" struct is composed of several substructures and those
  should be defined separately and used but the "super" struct.
  
  In the end we'll have an app that can read a full conversation with
  claude-code and display it. Long term this capability will be combined
  with the app repo and we'll create a coherent story of why, how and what
  for the app.

  Take a look at the file and let me know what you think

## Define serde structs for JSONL deserialization (20260323 0.1.0)

  Created `src/types.rs` with serde structs to deserialize all 5 JSONL record
  types from Claude Code session files:

  - `Record` — top-level enum, internally tagged on `"type"` field
  - `FileHistorySnapshotRecord` / `Snapshot` — file backup snapshots
  - `UserRecord` / `UserMessage` / `UserContent` — user prompts + tool results
  - `AssistantRecord` / `AssistantMessage` / `AssistantContentBlock` — model responses
  - `ProgressRecord` / `ProgressData` — hook execution events
  - `LastPromptRecord` — session end marker
  - `Usage` / `CacheCreation` — API usage metadata

  Key design decisions:
  - Internally tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) for `Record` and content blocks
  - `#[serde(untagged)]` for `UserContent` (string vs tool_result array)
  - `serde_json::Value` for polymorphic fields (`toolUseResult`, `tool_use.input`)
  - `Box<T>` on large enum variants per clippy `large_enum_variant`
  - camelCase rename on Claude Code wrapper structs; snake_case (default) on API structs

  Verified: all 22 lines of sample JSONL deserialize successfully.

Here's where things stand:

**Project**: `ccs-viewer` — a Rust tool to view Claude Code session JSONL files.

**Current version**: 0.1.0

**What's done**:
- Serde structs in `src/types.rs` for deserializing all 5 JSONL record types (user, assistant, progress, file history snapshot, last prompt)
- Sample JSONL data in `data/`

**Next todo**:
- "Have claude code design a claude-code session viewer" — the bigger goal of reading full conversations and displaying them

**Repo state**: Working copy (`@`) is empty on top of `main` at `ed4197c6`. Two jj repos (app + `.claude` session).

What would you like to work on?

     4→/// Top-level record: each line of a Claude Code session JSONL file
     5→/// deserializes into one of these variants, discriminated by the `type` field.
     6→#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
     7→#[serde(tag = "type")]
     8→pub enum Record {
     9→    #[serde(rename = "file-history-snapshot")]
    10→    FileHistorySnapshot(FileHistorySnapshotRecord),
    11→
    12→    #[serde(rename = "user")]
    13→    User(Box<UserRecord>),
    14→
    15→    #[serde(rename = "assistant")]
    16→    Assistant(Box<AssistantRecord>),
    17→
    18→    #[serde(rename = "progress")]
    19→    Progress(Box<ProgressRecord>),
    20→
    21→    #[serde(rename = "last-prompt")]
    22→    LastPrompt(LastPromptRecord),
    23→}
    24→

    79→/// User message content is either a plain text string (initial prompt)

    87→
    88→#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]

    96→
    97→/// Tool result content can be a plain string or a structured value.
    98→#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
    99→#[serde(untagged)]
   100→pub enum ToolResultContent {

   102→    Structured(Value),
   103→}

   109→#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
   110→#[serde(rename_all = "camelCase")]

   114→    pub message: AssistantMessage,
   115→    pub request_id: Option<String>,

   172→

   176→    pub ephemeral_5m_input_tokens: u64,

   178→    pub ephemeral_1h_input_tokens: u64,
   179→}
   180→
   181→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   182→// progress
   183→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   184→
   185→#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
   186→#[serde(rename_all = "camelCase")]
   187→pub struct ProgressRecord {
   188→    pub parent_uuid: String,
   189→    pub is_sidechain: bool,
   190→    pub data: ProgressData,
   191→    #[serde(rename = "parentToolUseID")]
   192→    pub parent_tool_use_id: Option<String>,
   193→    #[serde(rename = "toolUseID")]
   194→    pub tool_use_id: Option<String>,
   195→    pub timestamp: String,
   196→    pub uuid: String,
   197→    // Common metadata
   198→    pub user_type: Option<String>,
   199→    pub entrypoint: Option<String>,
   200→    pub cwd: Option<String>,
   201→    pub session_id: Option<String>,
   202→    pub version: Option<String>,
   203→    pub git_branch: Option<String>,
   204→    pub slug: Option<String>,
   205→}
   206→
   207→#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
   208→#[serde(rename_all = "camelCase")]
   209→pub struct ProgressData {
   210→    #[serde(rename = "type")]
   211→    pub data_type: String,
   212→    pub hook_event: Option<String>,
   213→    pub hook_name: Option<String>,
   214→    pub command: Option<String>,
   215→}
   216→
   217→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   218→// last-prompt
   219→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   220→
   221→#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
   222→#[serde(rename_all = "camelCase")]
   223→pub struct LastPromptRecord {
   224→    pub last_prompt: String,
   225→    pub session_id: String,
   226→}
   227→
   228→#[cfg(test)]
   229→mod tests {
   230→    use super::*;
   231→    use std::fs::File;
   232→    use std::io::{BufRead, BufReader};
   233→
   234→    #[test]
   235→    fn deserialize_sample_jsonl() {
   236→        let path = "hwr.claude/31ba272b-f7c2-436b-a017-269e27a64d07.jsonl";
   237→        let file = File::open(path).expect("sample JSONL file should exist");
   238→        let reader = BufReader::new(file);
   239→        let mut count = 0;
   240→        for (i, line) in reader.lines().enumerate() {
   241→            let line = line.unwrap();
   242→            if line.trim().is_empty() {
   243→                continue;
   244→            }
   245→            let result = serde_json::from_str::<Record>(&line);
   246→            assert!(
   247→                result.is_ok(),
   248→                "line {} failed: {}",
   249→                i + 1,
   250→                result.unwrap_err()
   251→            );
   252→            count += 1;
   253→        }
   254→        assert_eq!(count, 22);
   255→    }
   256→}

  Added 4 new variants to the `Record` enum discovered in the larger session
  file `data/997afb98-...jsonl` (461 records):

  - `QueueOperation` — slash command queue events (`enqueue`/`remove`)
  - `System` — turn duration metadata (subtype `turn_duration`)

  - `AgentName` — agent name assignment

# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Have claude code design a claude-code session viewer

  In `hwr.claude/` is a .jsonl file with a set of json lines representing me asking
  claude-code to "reqaquaint" (sp: reaquaint) itself with a trivial app created
  using, `cargo new hwr` and `hwr.claude/` is the directory `~/.claude/projects/<path>/`.

  Medium term want to convert the jsonl lines into a the "conversation".
  In the short term I'd like to create the rust structs that each represent
  the information, I don't want to process it I just want to be able to
  use serde to deserialize the data in the file into the set of rust structs.
  
  I think each line should destruct into a single struct, but I suspect
  this "super" struct is composed of several substructures and those
  should be defined separately and used but the "super" struct.
  
  In the end we'll have an app that can read a full conversation with
  claude-code and display it. Long term this capability will be combined
  with the app repo and we'll create a coherent story of why, how and what
  for the app.

  Take a look at the file and let me know what you think

## Define serde structs for JSONL deserialization (20260323 0.1.0)

  Created `src/types.rs` with serde structs to deserialize all 5 JSONL record
  types from Claude Code session files:

  - `Record` — top-level enum, internally tagged on `"type"` field
  - `FileHistorySnapshotRecord` / `Snapshot` — file backup snapshots
  - `UserRecord` / `UserMessage` / `UserContent` — user prompts + tool results
  - `AssistantRecord` / `AssistantMessage` / `AssistantContentBlock` — model responses
  - `ProgressRecord` / `ProgressData` — hook execution events
  - `LastPromptRecord` — session end marker
  - `Usage` / `CacheCreation` — API usage metadata

  Key design decisions:
  - Internally tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) for `Record` and content blocks
  - `#[serde(untagged)]` for `UserContent` (string vs tool_result array)
  - `serde_json::Value` for polymorphic fields (`toolUseResult`, `tool_use.input`)
  - `Box<T>` on large enum variants per clippy `large_enum_variant`
  - camelCase rename on Claude Code wrapper structs; snake_case (default) on API structs

  Verified: all 22 lines of sample JSONL deserialize successfully.

message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

<command-name>/effort</command-name>
            <command-message>effort</command-message>
            <command-args>max</command-args>
<local-command-stdout>Set effort level to max (this session only): Maximum capability with deepest reasoning (Opus 4.6 only)</local-command-stdout>
how did you decide which fields would be Option<xx> or not?

content.text:
Two factors:

1. **Empirical observation from the data.** I looked at the actual records. For `QueueOperationRecord`, the `enqueue` variant had `content` but `remove` did not — so `content: Option<String>`. For `CustomTitleRecord` and `AgentNameRecord`, both fields were present on all samples, so required.

2. **Copying the pattern from existing structs.** The common metadata fields (`user_type`, `entrypoint`, `cwd`, `session_id`, `version`, `git_branch`, `slug`) on `SystemRecord` are `Option` because that's what you already had on `UserRecord`, `AssistantRecord`, and `ProgressRecord`. I followed the established convention rather than re-evaluating.

The honest caveat: the sample sizes are tiny (2 `queue-operation`, 8 `system`, 2 each of `custom-title`/`agent-name`). A field that was always present in these samples might be absent in other sessions. For example, `SystemRecord::duration_ms` is required because all 8 records had it — but if there are other `subtype` values beyond `turn_duration`, they might not have that field. Making it `Option` or using `serde_json::Value` for the subtype-specific fields would be more defensive, but I went with the simpler approach given what we could see.

message.content: Just looking at user_type you've labeled it "Common metadata" I wonder it that should be a struct, just thinking?

content.text:
Yeah, those 7 fields (`user_type`, `entrypoint`, `cwd`, `session_id`, `version`, `git_branch`, `slug`) are repeated verbatim across `UserRecord`, `AssistantRecord`, `ProgressRecord`, and now `SystemRecord`. Extracting them into a shared struct would reduce duplication.

The main consideration is how serde handles it — you'd use `#[serde(flatten)]` to inline the fields during deserialization, which works well here since the metadata fields live at the same JSON level as the rest.

Something like:

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
#[serde(rename_all = "camelCase")]
pub struct SessionMetadata {
    pub user_type: Option<String>,
    pub entrypoint: Option<String>,
    pub cwd: Option<String>,
    pub session_id: Option<String>,
    pub version: Option<String>,
    pub git_branch: Option<String>,
    pub slug: Option<String>,
}

// then in each record:
#[serde(flatten)]
pub metadata: Option<SessionMetadata>,
// or just:
#[serde(flatten)]
pub metadata: SessionMetadata,
```

Want to do that now or save it for later?

message.content: I'm guessing the individual fields will always be present?

content.text:
Right — the struct itself is always present (the fields are always in the JSON), it's the individual field *values* that might be absent. So:

```rust
#[serde(flatten)]
pub metadata: SessionMetadata,
```

With the `Option`s staying on the individual fields inside `SessionMetadata`.

message.content: You think the individual fields would sometimes be absent?

content.text:
Good push. Looking at the data, every record that has those metadata fields has *all* of them populated. They were probably `Option` out of excessive caution. Making them all required `String` is likely correct — and if a future file proves otherwise, the deserialize error will tell us exactly which field was missing.

Want to include that refactor (extract `SessionMetadata` with required fields) in this version, or save it for the next one?

message.content: Let's add refactor SessionMetadata as the next todo and then commit + finalize this and try the refactor next

  In the short term I'd like to create the rust structs that each represent

## Define serde structs for JSONL deserialization (20260323 0.1.0)

There it is — a `text` block mixed in with `tool_result` blocks. Currently `UserContent::Blocks` expects `Vec<ToolResultBlock>`, but user content arrays can also contain `{"type": "text", "text": "..."}` entries.

The fix is to make the array items an enum that handles both `tool_result` and `text` block types. Single-step bump to **0.3.0**?

# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Have claude code design a claude-code session viewer

  In `hwr.claude/` is a .jsonl file with a set of json lines representing me asking
  claude-code to "reqaquaint" (sp: reaquaint) itself with a trivial app created
  using, `cargo new hwr` and `hwr.claude/` is the directory `~/.claude/projects/<path>/`.

  Medium term want to convert the jsonl lines into a the "conversation".
  In the short term I'd like to create the rust structs that each represent
  the information, I don't want to process it I just want to be able to
  use serde to deserialize the data in the file into the set of rust structs.
  
  I think each line should destruct into a single struct, but I suspect
  this "super" struct is composed of several substructures and those
  should be defined separately and used but the "super" struct.
  
  In the end we'll have an app that can read a full conversation with
  claude-code and display it. Long term this capability will be combined
  with the app repo and we'll create a coherent story of why, how and what
  for the app.

  Take a look at the file and let me know what you think

## Define serde structs for JSONL deserialization (20260323 0.1.0)

  Created `src/types.rs` with serde structs to deserialize all 5 JSONL record
  types from Claude Code session files:

  - `Record` — top-level enum, internally tagged on `"type"` field
  - `FileHistorySnapshotRecord` / `Snapshot` — file backup snapshots
  - `UserRecord` / `UserMessage` / `UserContent` — user prompts + tool results
  - `AssistantRecord` / `AssistantMessage` / `AssistantContentBlock` — model responses
  - `ProgressRecord` / `ProgressData` — hook execution events
  - `LastPromptRecord` — session end marker
  - `Usage` / `CacheCreation` — API usage metadata

  Key design decisions:
  - Internally tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) for `Record` and content blocks
  - `#[serde(untagged)]` for `UserContent` (string vs tool_result array)
  - `serde_json::Value` for polymorphic fields (`toolUseResult`, `tool_use.input`)
  - `Box<T>` on large enum variants per clippy `large_enum_variant`
  - camelCase rename on Claude Code wrapper structs; snake_case (default) on API structs

  Verified: all 22 lines of sample JSONL deserialize successfully.

## Add new record type variants (20260323 0.2.0)

  Added 4 new variants to the `Record` enum discovered in the larger session
  file `data/997afb98-...jsonl` (461 records):

  - `QueueOperation` — slash command queue events (`enqueue`/`remove`)
  - `System` — turn duration metadata (subtype `turn_duration`)
  - `CustomTitle` — user-set session title
  - `AgentName` — agent name assignment

  Also updated the test to cover both data files (moved path from
  `hwr.claude/` to `data/`) and added the new variant labels to `main.rs`.

## Refactor SessionMetadata

  Extract the 7 common metadata fields (`user_type`, `entrypoint`, `cwd`,
  `session_id`, `version`, `git_branch`, `slug`) repeated across
  `UserRecord`, `AssistantRecord`, `ProgressRecord`, and `SystemRecord`
  into a shared `SessionMetadata` struct using `#[serde(flatten)]`.
  Make individual fields required `String` instead of `Option<String>` —
  they appear on every record in the data we have.

lines: Make individual fields required `String` instead of `Option<String>` —
they appear on every record in the data we have.
+## Support text blocks in user content arrays (20260323 0.3.0)
+  User message content arrays can contain both `tool_result` and `text` blocks.
+  `UserContentBlock` is a tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) handling both
+  block types. Removed the standalone `ToolResultBlock` struct.
+  Discovered in session file `data/092de687-...jsonl` (line 81) where a user
+  follow-up text was interleaved with tool results in the same content array.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Have claude code design a claude-code session viewer

  In `hwr.claude/` is a .jsonl file with a set of json lines representing me asking
  claude-code to "reqaquaint" (sp: reaquaint) itself with a trivial app created
  using, `cargo new hwr` and `hwr.claude/` is the directory `~/.claude/projects/<path>/`.

  Medium term want to convert the jsonl lines into a the "conversation".
  In the short term I'd like to create the rust structs that each represent
  the information, I don't want to process it I just want to be able to
  use serde to deserialize the data in the file into the set of rust structs.
  
  I think each line should destruct into a single struct, but I suspect
  this "super" struct is composed of several substructures and those
  should be defined separately and used but the "super" struct.
  
  In the end we'll have an app that can read a full conversation with
  claude-code and display it. Long term this capability will be combined
  with the app repo and we'll create a coherent story of why, how and what
  for the app.

  Take a look at the file and let me know what you think

## Define serde structs for JSONL deserialization (20260323 0.1.0)

  Created `src/types.rs` with serde structs to deserialize all 5 JSONL record
  types from Claude Code session files:

  - `Record` — top-level enum, internally tagged on `"type"` field
  - `FileHistorySnapshotRecord` / `Snapshot` — file backup snapshots
  - `UserRecord` / `UserMessage` / `UserContent` — user prompts + tool results
  - `AssistantRecord` / `AssistantMessage` / `AssistantContentBlock` — model responses
  - `ProgressRecord` / `ProgressData` — hook execution events
  - `LastPromptRecord` — session end marker
  - `Usage` / `CacheCreation` — API usage metadata

  Key design decisions:
  - Internally tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) for `Record` and content blocks
  - `#[serde(untagged)]` for `UserContent` (string vs tool_result array)
  - `serde_json::Value` for polymorphic fields (`toolUseResult`, `tool_use.input`)
  - `Box<T>` on large enum variants per clippy `large_enum_variant`
  - camelCase rename on Claude Code wrapper structs; snake_case (default) on API structs

  Verified: all 22 lines of sample JSONL deserialize successfully.

## Add new record type variants (20260323 0.2.0)

  Added 4 new variants to the `Record` enum discovered in the larger session
  file `data/997afb98-...jsonl` (461 records):

  - `QueueOperation` — slash command queue events (`enqueue`/`remove`)
  - `System` — turn duration metadata (subtype `turn_duration`)
  - `CustomTitle` — user-set session title
  - `AgentName` — agent name assignment

  Also updated the test to cover both data files (moved path from
  `hwr.claude/` to `data/`) and added the new variant labels to `main.rs`.

## Refactor SessionMetadata

  Extract the 7 common metadata fields (`user_type`, `entrypoint`, `cwd`,
  `session_id`, `version`, `git_branch`, `slug`) repeated across
  `UserRecord`, `AssistantRecord`, `ProgressRecord`, and `SystemRecord`
  into a shared `SessionMetadata` struct using `#[serde(flatten)]`.
  Make individual fields required `String` instead of `Option<String>` —
  they appear on every record in the data we have.

## Support text blocks in user content arrays (20260323 0.3.0)

  User message content arrays can contain both `tool_result` and `text` blocks.
  Replaced `Vec<ToolResultBlock>` with `Vec<UserContentBlock>` where
  `UserContentBlock` is a tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) handling both
  block types. Removed the standalone `ToolResultBlock` struct.

  Discovered in session file `data/092de687-...jsonl` (line 81) where a user
  follow-up text was interleaved with tool results in the same content array.

lines: Discovered in session file `data/092de687-...jsonl` (line 81) where a user
follow-up text was interleaved with tool results in the same content array.
+  different fields. Made `duration_ms` optional (only on `turn_duration`) and

content.text:
Good idea. We could add a test that deserializes all files and asserts every `Record` variant appeared at least once. That way if a trimmed file accidentally drops coverage of a variant, the test catches it.

Something like collecting a `HashSet<&str>` of variant labels and asserting it equals the full set of 9 types. One test across all files rather than per-file — keeps it simple.

Want me to add that?

message.content: SG, also from the command line it would be nice to have -V for version and support wild cards, I assume not to hard?

content.text:
Not hard at all. The shell already expands wildcards, so `ccs-viewer data/*.jsonl` will pass multiple file args — we just need to iterate over all args instead of taking only the first. For `-V`, a simple check before processing.

Should I keep it lightweight (manual arg handling) or pull in `clap`? Manual is simpler for now, but clap would be cleaner if we expect more flags later.

message.content: Should we use clap?

content.text: I'd say yes — the app is growing and will likely gain more flags (output format, filtering, etc.). clap gives us `-V`/`--version` for free from `Cargo.toml`, proper `--help`, and clean multi-arg handling. The cost is one dependency but it's the standard choice for Rust CLIs.

message.content: agree

content.text:
This is a bigger change — version bump to **0.5.0**, single-step? Adding:

1. `clap` dependency with multiple file args + glob pattern support
2. `-V`/`--version` (free from clap)
3. Test asserting all 9 Record variants are covered

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Have claude code design a claude-code session viewer

  In `hwr.claude/` is a .jsonl file with a set of json lines representing me asking
  claude-code to "reqaquaint" (sp: reaquaint) itself with a trivial app created
  using, `cargo new hwr` and `hwr.claude/` is the directory `~/.claude/projects/<path>/`.

  Medium term want to convert the jsonl lines into a the "conversation".
  In the short term I'd like to create the rust structs that each represent
  the information, I don't want to process it I just want to be able to
  use serde to deserialize the data in the file into the set of rust structs.
  
  I think each line should destruct into a single struct, but I suspect
  this "super" struct is composed of several substructures and those
  should be defined separately and used but the "super" struct.
  
  In the end we'll have an app that can read a full conversation with
  claude-code and display it. Long term this capability will be combined
  with the app repo and we'll create a coherent story of why, how and what
  for the app.

  Take a look at the file and let me know what you think

## Define serde structs for JSONL deserialization (20260323 0.1.0)

  Created `src/types.rs` with serde structs to deserialize all 5 JSONL record
  types from Claude Code session files:

  - `Record` — top-level enum, internally tagged on `"type"` field
  - `FileHistorySnapshotRecord` / `Snapshot` — file backup snapshots
  - `UserRecord` / `UserMessage` / `UserContent` — user prompts + tool results
  - `AssistantRecord` / `AssistantMessage` / `AssistantContentBlock` — model responses
  - `ProgressRecord` / `ProgressData` — hook execution events
  - `LastPromptRecord` — session end marker
  - `Usage` / `CacheCreation` — API usage metadata

  Key design decisions:
  - Internally tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) for `Record` and content blocks
  - `#[serde(untagged)]` for `UserContent` (string vs tool_result array)
  - `serde_json::Value` for polymorphic fields (`toolUseResult`, `tool_use.input`)
  - `Box<T>` on large enum variants per clippy `large_enum_variant`
  - camelCase rename on Claude Code wrapper structs; snake_case (default) on API structs

  Verified: all 22 lines of sample JSONL deserialize successfully.

## Add new record type variants (20260323 0.2.0)

  Added 4 new variants to the `Record` enum discovered in the larger session
  file `data/997afb98-...jsonl` (461 records):

  - `QueueOperation` — slash command queue events (`enqueue`/`remove`)
  - `System` — turn duration metadata (subtype `turn_duration`)
  - `CustomTitle` — user-set session title
  - `AgentName` — agent name assignment

  Also updated the test to cover both data files (moved path from
  `hwr.claude/` to `data/`) and added the new variant labels to `main.rs`.

## Refactor SessionMetadata

  Extract the 7 common metadata fields (`user_type`, `entrypoint`, `cwd`,
  `session_id`, `version`, `git_branch`, `slug`) repeated across
  `UserRecord`, `AssistantRecord`, `ProgressRecord`, and `SystemRecord`
  into a shared `SessionMetadata` struct using `#[serde(flatten)]`.
  Make individual fields required `String` instead of `Option<String>` —
  they appear on every record in the data we have.

## Support text blocks in user content arrays (20260323 0.3.0)

  User message content arrays can contain both `tool_result` and `text` blocks.
  Replaced `Vec<ToolResultBlock>` with `Vec<UserContentBlock>` where
  `UserContentBlock` is a tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) handling both
  block types. Removed the standalone `ToolResultBlock` struct.

  Discovered in session file `data/092de687-...jsonl` (line 81) where a user
  follow-up text was interleaved with tool results in the same content array.

## Make SystemRecord subtype-specific fields optional (20260323 0.4.0)

  `SystemRecord` has multiple subtypes (`turn_duration`, `local_command`) with
  different fields. Made `duration_ms` optional (only on `turn_duration`) and
  added optional `content` and `level` fields (only on `local_command`).

  Discovered in `data/86fb7a89-...jsonl` (2222 records) where `local_command`
  system records lacked `durationMs`.

lines: +  Switch from manual arg parsing to `clap`. Gets us `-V`/`--version` for
+  free (from Cargo.toml), proper `--help`, and multi-file argument support.
+  Also add a test that asserts all 9 `Record` variants appear at least once
+  across test data files, so trimming a file doesn't silently drop coverage.

content.text:
There they are — `umqqovro` and `zovkznpu` both titled "fix: make SystemRecord subtype-specific fields optional (0.4.0)". The first one (`zovkznpu`) was the initial commit, the second (`umqqovro`) was after adding the todo/trimming.

But `main` is already pushed and finalized. Since these are immutable, we'd need `--ignore-immutable` to squash them. Want me to fix it, or leave it as-is since it's just the `.claude` repo?

message.content: fix it, no one else is using this. But why did it happen finalize is only on .claude and it should have squashed then pushed did you do 2 commits?

content.text:
Yes, I did two commits to `.claude` — the first at the 0.4.0 commit (`zovkznpu`), then a second one (`umqqovro`) after we added the todo and trimmed the file. I should have squashed the second into the first instead of creating a new commit. Same mistake as the 0.2.0 round.

The lesson: when adding more changes to `.claude` in the same logical step, squash into the existing `.claude` commit rather than creating a new one.
It opened vim because I didn't pass `-m`. Let me redo with a message:

added clap CLI todo.

ochid: /wotynpltrwym" \
-R .claude

toolUseResult.newString:
## Add clap CLI (20260323 0.5.0)

  Switched from manual arg parsing to `clap` (derive). Gets `-V`/`--version`
  for free from Cargo.toml, proper `--help`, and multi-file argument support
  (`ccs-viewer data/*.jsonl`).

  Added `Record::label()` method and `Record::all_labels()` to centralize
  variant label strings. Added `all_variants_covered` test that asserts every
  `Record` variant appears at least once across all test data files.
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Have claude code design a claude-code session viewer

  In `hwr.claude/` is a .jsonl file with a set of json lines representing me asking
  claude-code to "reqaquaint" (sp: reaquaint) itself with a trivial app created
  using, `cargo new hwr` and `hwr.claude/` is the directory `~/.claude/projects/<path>/`.

  Medium term want to convert the jsonl lines into a the "conversation".
  In the short term I'd like to create the rust structs that each represent
  the information, I don't want to process it I just want to be able to
  use serde to deserialize the data in the file into the set of rust structs.
  
  I think each line should destruct into a single struct, but I suspect
  this "super" struct is composed of several substructures and those
  should be defined separately and used but the "super" struct.
  
  In the end we'll have an app that can read a full conversation with
  claude-code and display it. Long term this capability will be combined
  with the app repo and we'll create a coherent story of why, how and what
  for the app.

  Take a look at the file and let me know what you think

## Define serde structs for JSONL deserialization (20260323 0.1.0)

  Created `src/types.rs` with serde structs to deserialize all 5 JSONL record
  types from Claude Code session files:

  - `Record` — top-level enum, internally tagged on `"type"` field
  - `FileHistorySnapshotRecord` / `Snapshot` — file backup snapshots
  - `UserRecord` / `UserMessage` / `UserContent` — user prompts + tool results
  - `AssistantRecord` / `AssistantMessage` / `AssistantContentBlock` — model responses
  - `ProgressRecord` / `ProgressData` — hook execution events
  - `LastPromptRecord` — session end marker
  - `Usage` / `CacheCreation` — API usage metadata

  Key design decisions:
  - Internally tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) for `Record` and content blocks
  - `#[serde(untagged)]` for `UserContent` (string vs tool_result array)
  - `serde_json::Value` for polymorphic fields (`toolUseResult`, `tool_use.input`)
  - `Box<T>` on large enum variants per clippy `large_enum_variant`
  - camelCase rename on Claude Code wrapper structs; snake_case (default) on API structs

  Verified: all 22 lines of sample JSONL deserialize successfully.

## Add new record type variants (20260323 0.2.0)

  Added 4 new variants to the `Record` enum discovered in the larger session
  file `data/997afb98-...jsonl` (461 records):

  - `QueueOperation` — slash command queue events (`enqueue`/`remove`)
  - `System` — turn duration metadata (subtype `turn_duration`)
  - `CustomTitle` — user-set session title
  - `AgentName` — agent name assignment

  Also updated the test to cover both data files (moved path from
  `hwr.claude/` to `data/`) and added the new variant labels to `main.rs`.

## Refactor SessionMetadata

  Extract the 7 common metadata fields (`user_type`, `entrypoint`, `cwd`,
  `session_id`, `version`, `git_branch`, `slug`) repeated across
  `UserRecord`, `AssistantRecord`, `ProgressRecord`, and `SystemRecord`
  into a shared `SessionMetadata` struct using `#[serde(flatten)]`.
  Make individual fields required `String` instead of `Option<String>` —
  they appear on every record in the data we have.

## Support text blocks in user content arrays (20260323 0.3.0)

  User message content arrays can contain both `tool_result` and `text` blocks.
  Replaced `Vec<ToolResultBlock>` with `Vec<UserContentBlock>` where
  `UserContentBlock` is a tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) handling both
  block types. Removed the standalone `ToolResultBlock` struct.

  Discovered in session file `data/092de687-...jsonl` (line 81) where a user
  follow-up text was interleaved with tool results in the same content array.

## Make SystemRecord subtype-specific fields optional (20260323 0.4.0)

  `SystemRecord` has multiple subtypes (`turn_duration`, `local_command`) with
  different fields. Made `duration_ms` optional (only on `turn_duration`) and
  added optional `content` and `level` fields (only on `local_command`).

  Discovered in `data/86fb7a89-...jsonl` (2222 records) where `local_command`
  system records lacked `durationMs`.

## Add clap CLI

  Switch from manual arg parsing to `clap`. Gets us `-V`/`--version` for
  free (from Cargo.toml), proper `--help`, and multi-file argument support.
  Also add a test that asserts all 9 `Record` variants appear at least once
  across test data files, so trimming a file doesn't silently drop coverage.

-  free (from Cargo.toml), proper `--help`, and multi-file argument support.
-  Also add a test that asserts all 9 `Record` variants appear at least once
-  across test data files, so trimming a file doesn't silently drop coverage.
+  Switched from manual arg parsing to `clap` (derive). Gets `-V`/`--version`
+  for free from Cargo.toml, proper `--help`, and multi-file argument support
+  variant label strings. Added `all_variants_covered` test that asserts every
+  `Record` variant appears at least once across all test data files.

message.content: Another test, on a run keep track of the number of times each field is used, I want to verify that all fields have at least one occurance, thoughts?

content.text:
Interesting idea. A few thoughts:

**What it would catch**: Fields that are *always* `None`/absent in our test data — meaning we've never actually verified serde can deserialize a real value into them. Essentially dead code in the structs.

**How to implement**: The simplest approach would be a test that deserializes all files and for each record, checks which `Option` fields are `Some` vs `None`, accumulating counts. Then assert every field has count > 0.

**The tricky part**: Getting field-level introspection without reflection. Rust doesn't have runtime reflection, so we'd need to either:
1. Write manual per-struct checking functions (tedious but explicit)
2. Deserialize to `serde_json::Value` in parallel and count which JSON keys appear (simpler, no struct coupling)
3. Use a derive macro (overkill)

I'd lean toward option 2 — deserialize each line as both `Record` (to verify it works) and `Value` (to collect field keys per record type). Then assert every field in our structs appears at least once in the `Value` data. No manual per-struct code needed, and it automatically adapts when we add fields.

Want to go with that approach?

message.content: Option 2 wouldn't handle cases like timestamp which turns up many times and I'd want to catch cases where it wasn't "tested" in a particular structure

content.text:
Good point. We need per-struct field tracking, not just global key counts. So `timestamp` in `SystemRecord` is tracked separately from `timestamp` in `QueueOperationRecord`.

Option 2 still works — we just key on `(record_type, field_name)`. Deserialize each line as `Value`, read the `"type"` field to get the record type, then collect all keys present. At the end, compare against expected fields per struct.

The expected fields could be a static map like:

```rust
("system", &["parentUuid", "isSidechain", "subtype", "timestamp", ...]),
("queue-operation", &["operation", "timestamp", "sessionId", ...]),
```

That does duplicate the struct definitions, but it's the test's job to verify them — and if someone adds a field to the struct but not the test, the test won't fail (safe direction). If someone adds a field to the test list but it never appears, the test catches it.

Want me to add this as part of 0.5.0 (squash in) or as a new version?

message.content: The case of forgetting to add it to a test, feels like a false sense of secturity although not catastrophic as it is still being used. In the case of timestamp it's not optional if it's absent it'll fail at runtime?

content.text:
Right. Required fields (`String`, `u64`, etc.) are already covered — if they're absent, serde fails and our existing tests catch that. The only fields worth tracking are `Option` ones, since those silently succeed as `None` whether or not we've ever seen a real value.

So the test narrows to: for each `Option` field in each struct, verify it's `Some` at least once across test data. That's the gap our current tests don't cover.

And for the "forgot to add to test" problem — we could flip it: instead of a static expected list, derive the `Option` field names from the `Value` approach. Look at all keys that *do* appear for a record type across all files, and compare against keys that appear in *every* record of that type. The difference tells us which fields are sometimes absent (i.e., `Option`). If an `Option` field is *never* present, that's the warning.

Though honestly the simpler version — a static list of `Option` fields per record type — is clear, easy to maintain, and the failure mode (forgetting to add a new field) is benign. Want to go with that?

message.content: Ok the static list but add a warning in the code it doesn't catch fields that are always None if no test

content.text: Single-step **0.6.0** for this?

message.content: y

message.content: Thought, can we move all the Option after the non-Options, seems possible if serde doesn't care about order?

content.text:
Correct — serde doesn't care about field order when deserializing JSON. We can group all `Option` fields at the bottom of each struct. That makes it visually obvious which fields need entries in `optional_fields()`.

Want me to do that reorganization as part of this 0.6.0 change?

message.content: Y

    44→            Record::Progress(_) => "progress",

    50→        }

   362→#[cfg(test)]
   363→mod tests {
   364→    use super::*;
   365→    use std::collections::{HashMap, HashSet};
   366→    use std::fs::File;
   367→    use std::io::{BufRead, BufReader};
   368→
   369→    const TEST_FILES: &[&str] = &[
   370→        "data/31ba272b-f7c2-436b-a017-269e27a64d07.jsonl",
   371→        "data/997afb98-c6aa-4f4a-92ac-a841e040414b.jsonl",
   372→        "data/092de687-cd0d-4583-b872-bc2908dff3ba.jsonl",
   373→        "data/86fb7a89-abfa-4e84-b862-5983e93c0b3b.jsonl",
   374→    ];
   375→
   376→    fn deserialize_file(path: &str) -> usize {
   377→        deserialize_file_collecting(path, None)
   378→    }
   379→
   380→    fn deserialize_file_collecting(path: &str, mut labels: Option<&mut HashSet<String>>) -> usize {
   381→        let file = File::open(path).expect("sample JSONL file should exist");
   382→        let reader = BufReader::new(file);
   383→        let mut count = 0;
   384→        for (i, line) in reader.lines().enumerate() {
   385→            let line = line.unwrap();
   386→            if line.trim().is_empty() {
   387→                continue;
   388→            }
   389→            let result = serde_json::from_str::<Record>(&line);
   390→            assert!(
   391→                result.is_ok(),
   392→                "line {} failed: {}",
   393→                i + 1,
   394→                result.unwrap_err()
   395→            );
   396→            if let (Ok(record), Some(labels)) = (&result, labels.as_deref_mut()) {
   397→                labels.insert(record.label().to_string());
   398→            }
   399→            count += 1;
   400→        }
   401→        count
   402→    }
   403→
   404→    #[test]
   405→    fn deserialize_sample_31ba() {
   406→        assert_eq!(
   407→            deserialize_file("data/31ba272b-f7c2-436b-a017-269e27a64d07.jsonl"),
   408→            22
   409→        );
   410→    }
   411→
   412→    #[test]
   413→    fn deserialize_sample_997a() {
   414→        assert_eq!(
   415→            deserialize_file("data/997afb98-c6aa-4f4a-92ac-a841e040414b.jsonl"),
   416→            461
   417→        );
   418→    }
   419→
   420→    #[test]
   421→    fn deserialize_sample_092d() {
   422→        assert_eq!(
   423→            deserialize_file("data/092de687-cd0d-4583-b872-bc2908dff3ba.jsonl"),
   424→            223
   425→        );
   426→    }
   427→
   428→    #[test]
   429→    fn deserialize_sample_86fb() {
   430→        assert_eq!(
   431→            deserialize_file("data/86fb7a89-abfa-4e84-b862-5983e93c0b3b.jsonl"),
   432→            301
   433→        );
   434→    }
   435→
   436→    #[test]
   437→    fn all_variants_covered() {
   438→        let mut seen = HashSet::new();
   439→        for path in TEST_FILES {
   440→            deserialize_file_collecting(path, Some(&mut seen));
   441→        }
   442→        let expected: HashSet<String> =
   443→            Record::all_labels().iter().map(|s| s.to_string()).collect();
   444→        let missing: Vec<_> = expected.difference(&seen).collect();
   445→        assert!(
   446→            missing.is_empty(),
   447→            "Record variants not covered by test data: {missing:?}"
   448→        );
   449→    }
   450→}
   451→

content.text:
There it is — line 588 has `"sourceToolAssistantUUID"` (note: all caps `UUID`, not `Uuid`). It's in the full `.claude/092de687` file which is *this current session*. And the key is `sourceToolAssistantUUID` not `sourceToolAssistantUuid` — that's a casing mismatch with `camelCase` rename. Let me check if our struct field would even match:

The struct has `source_tool_assistant_uuid` with `rename_all = "camelCase"`, which produces `sourceToolAssistantUuid`. But the JSON key is `sourceToolAssistantUUID`. That would mean serde *never* matches it — it silently falls through as `None`.

We need a `#[serde(rename = "sourceToolAssistantUUID")]` override. And the data file for testing needs to include those lines. Let me recopy the current session file (truncated to fit under 1 MiB) to replace the `data/092de687` file since it now has the field.

`stop_sequence` is always `null` across all test data — it's only populated when the model stops due to a custom stop sequence, which Claude Code doesn't use. This is a genuinely untested field. It exists in the API spec but we have no test data for it.

Options:
1. Remove it from the `optional_fields` list (accepting it's untested, with a comment)
2. Create a synthetic test line
3. Leave it and mark the test as expecting this known gap

I'd suggest option 1 — remove from the list with a comment noting it's always null in practice:

file.content:
Switched from manual arg parsing to `clap` (derive). Gets `-V`/`--version`
  for free from Cargo.toml, proper `--help`, and multi-file argument support
  (`ccs-viewer data/*.jsonl`).

  Added `Record::label()` method and `Record::all_labels()` to centralize
  variant label strings. Added `all_variants_covered` test that asserts every
  `Record` variant appears at least once across all test data files.

input.old_string:
Added `Record::label()` method and `Record::all_labels()` to centralize
  variant label strings. Added `all_variants_covered` test that asserts every
  `Record` variant appears at least once across all test data files.
input.new_string:
Added `Record::label()` method and `Record::all_labels()` to centralize
  variant label strings. Added `all_variants_covered` test that asserts every
  `Record` variant appears at least once across all test data files.

## Add all-optional-fields-seen test (20260323 0.6.0)

  Added `all_optional_fields_seen` test that verifies every `Option` field
  in every struct is `Some` at least once across test data. Each struct with
  Option fields has an `optional_fields()` method listing camelCase JSON names
  right next to the struct definition. Nested fields use dot notation
  (e.g. `"message.usage.speed"`), array filtering uses bracket notation
  (e.g. `"message.content[tool_use].caller"`).

  Reordered struct fields: required fields first, Option fields grouped at
  the bottom with a separator comment.

  Found and fixed a real bug: `sourceToolAssistantUUID` was never being
  deserialized because `rename_all = "camelCase"` produced
  `sourceToolAssistantUuid` but the JSON key uses all-caps `UUID`.
  Added explicit `#[serde(rename = "sourceToolAssistantUUID")]`.

  `stop_sequence` is excluded from the test list — always null in practice
  (custom stop sequences are not used by Claude Code).

  (e.g. `"message.content[tool_use].caller"`).

  Reordered struct fields: required fields first, Option fields grouped at
  the bottom with a separator comment.

  Found and fixed a real bug: `sourceToolAssistantUUID` was never being
  deserialized because `rename_all = "camelCase"` produced
  `sourceToolAssistantUuid` but the JSON key uses all-caps `UUID`.
  Added explicit `#[serde(rename = "sourceToolAssistantUUID")]`.

  `stop_sequence` is excluded from the test list — always null in practice
  (custom stop sequences are not used by Claude Code).
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Have claude code design a claude-code session viewer

  In `hwr.claude/` is a .jsonl file with a set of json lines representing me asking
  claude-code to "reqaquaint" (sp: reaquaint) itself with a trivial app created
  using, `cargo new hwr` and `hwr.claude/` is the directory `~/.claude/projects/<path>/`.

  Medium term want to convert the jsonl lines into a the "conversation".
  In the short term I'd like to create the rust structs that each represent
  the information, I don't want to process it I just want to be able to
  use serde to deserialize the data in the file into the set of rust structs.
  
  I think each line should destruct into a single struct, but I suspect
  this "super" struct is composed of several substructures and those
  should be defined separately and used but the "super" struct.
  
  In the end we'll have an app that can read a full conversation with
  claude-code and display it. Long term this capability will be combined
  with the app repo and we'll create a coherent story of why, how and what
  for the app.

  Take a look at the file and let me know what you think

## Define serde structs for JSONL deserialization (20260323 0.1.0)

  Created `src/types.rs` with serde structs to deserialize all 5 JSONL record
  types from Claude Code session files:

  - `Record` — top-level enum, internally tagged on `"type"` field
  - `FileHistorySnapshotRecord` / `Snapshot` — file backup snapshots
  - `UserRecord` / `UserMessage` / `UserContent` — user prompts + tool results
  - `AssistantRecord` / `AssistantMessage` / `AssistantContentBlock` — model responses
  - `ProgressRecord` / `ProgressData` — hook execution events
  - `LastPromptRecord` — session end marker
  - `Usage` / `CacheCreation` — API usage metadata

  Key design decisions:
  - Internally tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) for `Record` and content blocks
  - `#[serde(untagged)]` for `UserContent` (string vs tool_result array)
  - `serde_json::Value` for polymorphic fields (`toolUseResult`, `tool_use.input`)
  - `Box<T>` on large enum variants per clippy `large_enum_variant`
  - camelCase rename on Claude Code wrapper structs; snake_case (default) on API structs

  Verified: all 22 lines of sample JSONL deserialize successfully.

## Add new record type variants (20260323 0.2.0)

  Added 4 new variants to the `Record` enum discovered in the larger session
  file `data/997afb98-...jsonl` (461 records):

  - `QueueOperation` — slash command queue events (`enqueue`/`remove`)
  - `System` — turn duration metadata (subtype `turn_duration`)
  - `CustomTitle` — user-set session title
  - `AgentName` — agent name assignment

  Also updated the test to cover both data files (moved path from
  `hwr.claude/` to `data/`) and added the new variant labels to `main.rs`.

## Refactor SessionMetadata

  Extract the 7 common metadata fields (`user_type`, `entrypoint`, `cwd`,
  `session_id`, `version`, `git_branch`, `slug`) repeated across
  `UserRecord`, `AssistantRecord`, `ProgressRecord`, and `SystemRecord`
  into a shared `SessionMetadata` struct using `#[serde(flatten)]`.
  Make individual fields required `String` instead of `Option<String>` —
  they appear on every record in the data we have.

## Support text blocks in user content arrays (20260323 0.3.0)

  User message content arrays can contain both `tool_result` and `text` blocks.
  Replaced `Vec<ToolResultBlock>` with `Vec<UserContentBlock>` where
  `UserContentBlock` is a tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) handling both
  block types. Removed the standalone `ToolResultBlock` struct.

  Discovered in session file `data/092de687-...jsonl` (line 81) where a user
  follow-up text was interleaved with tool results in the same content array.

## Make SystemRecord subtype-specific fields optional (20260323 0.4.0)

  `SystemRecord` has multiple subtypes (`turn_duration`, `local_command`) with
  different fields. Made `duration_ms` optional (only on `turn_duration`) and
  added optional `content` and `level` fields (only on `local_command`).

  Discovered in `data/86fb7a89-...jsonl` (2222 records) where `local_command`
  system records lacked `durationMs`.

## Add clap CLI (20260323 0.5.0)

  Switched from manual arg parsing to `clap` (derive). Gets `-V`/`--version`
  for free from Cargo.toml, proper `--help`, and multi-file argument support
  (`ccs-viewer data/*.jsonl`).

  Added `Record::label()` method and `Record::all_labels()` to centralize
  variant label strings. Added `all_variants_covered` test that asserts every
  `Record` variant appears at least once across all test data files.

lines: variant label strings. Added `all_variants_covered` test that asserts every
`Record` variant appears at least once across all test data files.
+  Added `all_optional_fields_seen` test that verifies every `Option` field
+  in every struct is `Some` at least once across test data. Each struct with
+  Option fields has an `optional_fields()` method listing camelCase JSON names
+  right next to the struct definition. Nested fields use dot notation
+  Reordered struct fields: required fields first, Option fields grouped at
+  the bottom with a separator comment.
+  Found and fixed a real bug: `sourceToolAssistantUUID` was never being
+  `sourceToolAssistantUuid` but the JSON key uses all-caps `UUID`.
+  `stop_sequence` is excluded from the test list — always null in practice
+  (custom stop sequences are not used by Claude Code).

content.text:
There were new fields in the JSON (`container`, `isApiErrorMessage`, `context_management`), but serde's default behavior is `deny_unknown_fields` is **off** — it silently ignores JSON keys that don't map to a struct field. So they didn't cause errors, but they also aren't captured in our structs.

Whether we should add them depends on whether we'll need them. Want to add them now, or note them as a future TODO?

message.content: we'll need "every" field possible, but we don't have to have that now but since we have a line with these new fields we "must" add them I vote turning on "deny_unknown_fields" so we absolutely know there is a problem with our code.

content.text: Great call. Let me add `deny_unknown_fields` and let the tests tell us what's missing.

file.content:
use serde::Deserialize;
use serde_json::Value;

/// Top-level record: each line of a Claude Code session JSONL file
/// deserializes into one of these variants, discriminated by the `type` field.
#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
#[serde(tag = "type")]
pub enum Record {
    #[serde(rename = "file-history-snapshot")]
    FileHistorySnapshot(FileHistorySnapshotRecord),

    #[serde(rename = "user")]
    User(Box<UserRecord>),

    #[serde(rename = "assistant")]
    Assistant(Box<AssistantRecord>),

    #[serde(rename = "progress")]
    Progress(Box<ProgressRecord>),

    #[serde(rename = "last-prompt")]
    LastPrompt(LastPromptRecord),

    #[serde(rename = "queue-operation")]
    QueueOperation(QueueOperationRecord),

    #[serde(rename = "system")]
    System(Box<SystemRecord>),

    #[serde(rename = "custom-title")]
    CustomTitle(CustomTitleRecord),

    #[serde(rename = "agent-name")]
    AgentName(AgentNameRecord),
}

impl Record {
    /// Returns the type label string for this record variant.
    pub fn label(&self) -> &'static str {
        match self {
            Record::FileHistorySnapshot(_) => "file-history-snapshot",
            Record::User(_) => "user",
            Record::Assistant(_) => "assistant",
            Record::Progress(_) => "progress",
            Record::LastPrompt(_) => "last-prompt",
            Record::QueueOperation(_) => "queue-operation",
            Record::System(_) => "system",
            Record::CustomTitle(_) => "custom-title",
            Record::AgentName(_) => "agent-name",
        }

2. **UserRecord** has `isMeta` field we don't know about

## Add all-optional-fields-seen test (20260323 0.6.0)

  Added `all_optional_fields_seen` test that verifies every `Option` field
  in every struct is `Some` at least once across test data. Each struct with
  Option fields has an `optional_fields()` method listing camelCase JSON names
  right next to the struct definition. Nested fields use dot notation
  (e.g. `"message.usage.speed"`), array filtering uses bracket notation
  (e.g. `"message.content[tool_use].caller"`).

  Reordered struct fields: required fields first, Option fields grouped at
  the bottom with a separator comment.

  Found and fixed a real bug: `sourceToolAssistantUUID` was never being
  deserialized because `rename_all = "camelCase"` produced
  `sourceToolAssistantUuid` but the JSON key uses all-caps `UUID`.
  Added explicit `#[serde(rename = "sourceToolAssistantUUID")]`.

  `stop_sequence` is excluded from the test list — always null in practice
  (custom stop sequences are not used by Claude Code).

# Chores-01

Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
and how this task will be completed.

See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)

## Have claude code design a claude-code session viewer

  In `hwr.claude/` is a .jsonl file with a set of json lines representing me asking
  claude-code to "reqaquaint" (sp: reaquaint) itself with a trivial app created
  using, `cargo new hwr` and `hwr.claude/` is the directory `~/.claude/projects/<path>/`.

  Medium term want to convert the jsonl lines into a the "conversation".
  In the short term I'd like to create the rust structs that each represent
  the information, I don't want to process it I just want to be able to
  use serde to deserialize the data in the file into the set of rust structs.
  
  I think each line should destruct into a single struct, but I suspect
  this "super" struct is composed of several substructures and those
  should be defined separately and used but the "super" struct.
  
  In the end we'll have an app that can read a full conversation with
  claude-code and display it. Long term this capability will be combined
  with the app repo and we'll create a coherent story of why, how and what
  for the app.

  Take a look at the file and let me know what you think

## Define serde structs for JSONL deserialization (20260323 0.1.0)

  Created `src/types.rs` with serde structs to deserialize all 5 JSONL record
  types from Claude Code session files:

  - `Record` — top-level enum, internally tagged on `"type"` field
  - `FileHistorySnapshotRecord` / `Snapshot` — file backup snapshots
  - `UserRecord` / `UserMessage` / `UserContent` — user prompts + tool results
  - `AssistantRecord` / `AssistantMessage` / `AssistantContentBlock` — model responses
  - `ProgressRecord` / `ProgressData` — hook execution events
  - `LastPromptRecord` — session end marker
  - `Usage` / `CacheCreation` — API usage metadata

  Key design decisions:
  - Internally tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) for `Record` and content blocks
  - `#[serde(untagged)]` for `UserContent` (string vs tool_result array)
  - `serde_json::Value` for polymorphic fields (`toolUseResult`, `tool_use.input`)
  - `Box<T>` on large enum variants per clippy `large_enum_variant`
  - camelCase rename on Claude Code wrapper structs; snake_case (default) on API structs

  Verified: all 22 lines of sample JSONL deserialize successfully.

## Add new record type variants (20260323 0.2.0)

  Added 4 new variants to the `Record` enum discovered in the larger session
  file `data/997afb98-...jsonl` (461 records):

  - `QueueOperation` — slash command queue events (`enqueue`/`remove`)
  - `System` — turn duration metadata (subtype `turn_duration`)
  - `CustomTitle` — user-set session title
  - `AgentName` — agent name assignment

  Also updated the test to cover both data files (moved path from
  `hwr.claude/` to `data/`) and added the new variant labels to `main.rs`.

## Refactor SessionMetadata

  Extract the 7 common metadata fields (`user_type`, `entrypoint`, `cwd`,
  `session_id`, `version`, `git_branch`, `slug`) repeated across
  `UserRecord`, `AssistantRecord`, `ProgressRecord`, and `SystemRecord`
  into a shared `SessionMetadata` struct using `#[serde(flatten)]`.
  Make individual fields required `String` instead of `Option<String>` —
  they appear on every record in the data we have.

## Support text blocks in user content arrays (20260323 0.3.0)

  User message content arrays can contain both `tool_result` and `text` blocks.
  Replaced `Vec<ToolResultBlock>` with `Vec<UserContentBlock>` where
  `UserContentBlock` is a tagged enum (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) handling both
  block types. Removed the standalone `ToolResultBlock` struct.

  Discovered in session file `data/092de687-...jsonl` (line 81) where a user
  follow-up text was interleaved with tool results in the same content array.

## Make SystemRecord subtype-specific fields optional (20260323 0.4.0)

  `SystemRecord` has multiple subtypes (`turn_duration`, `local_command`) with
  different fields. Made `duration_ms` optional (only on `turn_duration`) and
  added optional `content` and `level` fields (only on `local_command`).

  Discovered in `data/86fb7a89-...jsonl` (2222 records) where `local_command`
  system records lacked `durationMs`.

## Add clap CLI (20260323 0.5.0)

  Switched from manual arg parsing to `clap` (derive). Gets `-V`/`--version`
  for free from Cargo.toml, proper `--help`, and multi-file argument support
  (`ccs-viewer data/*.jsonl`).

  Added `Record::label()` method and `Record::all_labels()` to centralize
  variant label strings. Added `all_variants_covered` test that asserts every
  `Record` variant appears at least once across all test data files.

## Add all-optional-fields-seen test (20260323 0.6.0)

  Added `all_optional_fields_seen` test that verifies every `Option` field
  in every struct is `Some` at least once across test data. Each struct with
  Option fields has an `optional_fields()` method listing camelCase JSON names
  right next to the struct definition. Nested fields use dot notation
  (e.g. `"message.usage.speed"`), array filtering uses bracket notation
  (e.g. `"message.content[tool_use].caller"`).

  Reordered struct fields: required fields first, Option fields grouped at
  the bottom with a separator comment.

  Found and fixed a real bug: `sourceToolAssistantUUID` was never being
  deserialized because `rename_all = "camelCase"` produced
  `sourceToolAssistantUuid` but the JSON key uses all-caps `UUID`.
  Added explicit `#[serde(rename = "sourceToolAssistantUUID")]`.

  `stop_sequence` is excluded from the test list — always null in practice
  (custom stop sequences are not used by Claude Code).

message.content: looking at impl Record::label and Record::all_labels the have the same set of strings, do we need both?

content.text:
Good catch. `all_labels()` could just be derived from calling `label()` on each variant. But since we don't have instances of each variant lying around, the simplest approach is to keep `all_labels()` and have `label()` index into it. Or — we could drop `all_labels()` and have the `all_variants_covered` test build the expected set differently.

Actually, the cleanest fix: keep `all_labels()` as the single source of truth, and make `label()` return from that same list. But matching an enum variant to an index is awkward.

Simplest: drop `all_labels()`, and in the test just hardcode the expected count (9 variants) instead of comparing against a list. Or have the test use `Record::optional_fields_by_type()` keys plus the types that have no optional fields.

What do you think — drop `all_labels()` and just assert `seen.len() == 9` in the test?

message.content: I if both are deseriable I'd have label use all_labels so as we DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself), thoughts

content.text:
I'd add a module-level doc comment at the top of `types.rs` — a short overview explaining:
- What the file does (deserializes Claude Code session JSONL)
- The `Record` enum pattern (internally tagged on `"type"`)
- The `RECORD_LABELS` / `label()` / `all_labels()` DRY pattern
- The `deny_unknown_fields` + `optional_fields()` testing strategy
- The struct layout convention (required fields first, Option fields at bottom)

That way the docs live right where someone opens the file. When we eventually refactor into submodules, the docs move with the code.

For `notes/README.md`, a brief mention that `types.rs` has the module docs would suffice — no need to duplicate.

lines: +//! Serde types for Claude Code session JSONL files.
+//! Each line of a session file is a JSON object with a `"type"` field that
+//! determines its shape. The [`Record`] enum uses serde's internally tagged
+//! representation (`#[serde(tag = "type")]`) to dispatch to the correct
+//! **Label strings** — The `RECORD_LABELS` const is the single source of
+//! the full slice. This avoids duplicating strings between the two methods.
+//! **Struct field ordering** — Each struct lists required fields first,
+//! then `Option` fields below a separator comment. This makes it visually
+//! obvious which fields need entries in the `optional_fields()` list.
+//! so that any JSON key not mapped to a Rust field causes a deserialization
+//! error. This ensures we know immediately when Claude Code adds new fields.
+//! **`optional_fields()`** — Structs with `Option` fields have a companion
+//! `optional_fields()` method listing the camelCase (or snake_case) JSON
+//! names of those fields. The `all_optional_fields_seen` test uses these
+//! lists to verify every `Option` field is `Some` at least once in test
+//! data. Nested fields use dot notation (`"message.usage.speed"`) and
+//! **Warning**: The `optional_fields()` lists are static — adding an
+//! `Option` field to a struct without updating the list means that field
+//! silently goes untested. The separator comment in each struct serves as

file.content:
# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions

## Memory vs CLAUDE.md

Prefer CLAUDE.md for project instructions and conventions — it is
checked into the repo and portable. Use memory/ only for user-specific
context that does not belong in the codebase.

## Project Structure

This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:

1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.

Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.

## Repo Paths (relative from project root)

- App repo: `.` (project root)
- Bot session repo: `.claude`
  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)

## Working Directory

Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
command failures downstream.

## Committing

Use `-R` (`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the verb/action
visible at the start of the command.

### App repo
```
jj commit -m \
"title" \
-m "body

ochid: /.claude/<changeID>" \
-R .
```

### Bot session repo
```
jj commit -m \
"title" \
-m "body

ochid: /<changeID>" \
-R .claude
```

## jj Basics

- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
  session because session data updates continuously.

## Commit Message Style

Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
- **Body**: expand on *what* if needed, plus short *why* and *how*.
- Examples:
  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`

## Pre-commit Requirements

### User approval

Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.

### Notes references

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.

### Versioning

Every change must start with a version bump. See
[Versioning during development](notes/README.md#versioning-during-development)
for details. Get user approval on single-step vs multi-step before starting.

### Pre-commit checklist

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path .` (if applicable)
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
7. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
8. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
   new subcommands, changed behavior)

## ochid Trailers

Every commit body must include an `ochid:` trailer pointing to the
counterpart commit in the other repo. The value is a workspace-root-relative
path followed by the **12-character** changeID (the full short form from
`vc-x1 chid -L`, not the truncated prefix jj shows in logs):

- App repo commits point to `.claude`: `ochid: /.claude/<changeID>`
- Bot session commits point to app repo: `ochid: /<changeID>`

Use `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` to get both 12-character changeIDs
(first line is app repo, second is `.claude`).

## Session End Workflows

When the user asks to "commit both repos" or says they're done, commit
both repos. Use the **same title** for both commits so they're easy to
correlate. The body can differ: the app repo body should summarize code
changes; the bot session repo body should note what was done in the
session.

```
jj commit -m "shared title" -m "app body" -R .
jj commit -m "shared title" -m "session body" -R .claude
```

After both commits, **pause and ask for user approval** before
proceeding to bookmark/push/finalize.

When the user also asks to push, advance the current bookmark on both
repos, then push the app repo. Do **not** push `.claude` here —
`finalize` handles that push after squashing trailing writes.

Replace `<bookmark>` with the active bookmark (e.g. `main`,
`dev-0.14.0`).

```
jj bookmark set <bookmark> -r @- -R .
jj bookmark set <bookmark> -r @- -R .claude
jj git push --bookmark <bookmark> -R .
```

### Late changes after push

If changes are made to the app repo after it has been pushed (e.g.
updating CLAUDE.md or memory), the commit is now immutable. Use
`--ignore-immutable` to squash the changes in, then re-push:

```
jj squash --ignore-immutable -R .
jj bookmark set <bookmark> -r @- -R .
jj git push --bookmark <bookmark> -R .
```

### Finalize the .claude repo

The **very last action** in a session is to finalize the `.claude` repo.
This squashes the working copy into the session commit and pushes. The
delay gives a safety margin against any pending writes. Always use a
short relative path for `--repo`.

**Nothing should happen after finalize** — no memory writes, no tool
calls, no additional output. If any work is done after finalize, run
finalize again so the trailing writes are captured.

`--bookmark` is required — use the active bookmark for the session.

```
vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --bookmark <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --push
```

Do **not** echo or restate the finalize output — the Bash tool
already displays it. Any trailing text output creates writes that
miss the finalize squash window.
